@@ -0,0 +1,345 @@

+
+import os
+import inspect
+from typing import Any, Union, Mapping, Callable, Awaitable
+from typing_extensions import Self, override
+
+import httpx
+

+from .._client import OpenAI, AsyncOpenAI
+from .._exceptions import OpenAIError
+from .._base_client import DEFAULT_MAX_RETRIES
+
+# Sentinel API key used when SigV4 mode is active, so the base OpenAI

+API_KEY_SENTINEL = "<bedrock-mantle-sigv4>"
discussion_comments.comment_body: This looks fragile. If a user happens to pass this exact string as api_key, it silently activates SigV4 mode instead of using the key. Consider making it private (_API_KEY_SENTINEL) and using a less guessable value (e.g. a UUID) to reduce the chance of accidental collision.

discussion_comments.comment_body: Thanks, this makes sense. I'll update the implementation so callable API keys are only refreshed when the request actually falls back to API-key auth, and I'll add coverage for the Azure AD token path as well.

Thanks, updated this so callable API keys are only refreshed on the API-key auth path. I also added sync and async regression tests to cover the Azure AD token path and ensure the API key provider is not called when AD auth is used.

commented_code:
with caplog.at_level(logging.DEBUG):
         logger_with_filter.debug("Sending HTTP Request: %s %s", "POST", "chat/completions")
     assert caplog.messages[0] == "Sending HTTP Request: POST chat/completions"
+
+
+def test_response_headers_redaction() -> None:
+    """Verify that filtering response headers with SENSITIVE_HEADERS redacts sensitive values."""
+    raw_headers = {
+        "content-type": "application/json",
+        "authorization": "Bearer sk-secret-key",
+        "api-key": "my-secret-api-key",
+        "x-request-id": "req_abc123",
+    }
+    filtered = {
+        k: v if k.lower() not in SENSITIVE_HEADERS else "<redacted>"
+        for k, v in raw_headers.items()
+    }

+def test_response_headers_redaction() -> None:

+    raw_headers = {
+        "content-type": "application/json",
+        "authorization": "Bearer sk-secret-key",
+        "api-key": "my-secret-api-key",
+        "x-request-id": "req_abc123",
+    }

+    }

discussion_comments.commented_code:
@@ -79,6 +81,7 @@
 class OpenAI(SyncAPIClient):
     # client options
     api_key: str
+    bearer_token_provider: Callable[[], str] | None = None
discussion_comments.comment_body:
I don't think this should be public, instead something like?
```suggestion
    _api_key_provider: Callable[[], str] | None = None
```

discussion_comments.commented_code:
@@ -79,6 +81,7 @@
 class OpenAI(SyncAPIClient):
     # client options
     api_key: str
+    bearer_token_provider: Callable[[], str] | None = None
discussion_comments.comment_body:
Making it public allows the same users that read the api_key attribute to get an updated bearer token. But I also don't think that (beyond debugging) there are that many scenarios where you would want to do that. So I have no problems with making it private. 

If I do follow that train of thought (developers are unlikely to read the api_key off the client in "real" production code) all the way through, I could make the argument that while it would technically be breaking to make the api_key property on the client a `str | Callable[[], str]`, it would simplify things by having a single place to store what is currently mutually exclusive settings.

+class APIKeyFilter(logging.Filter):

+                for header in logged_headers:
+                    if header.lower() in ["api-key", "authorization"]:
+                        logged_headers[header] = "<redacted>"

discussion_comments.comment_body:
also I haven't actually written a log filter before, is it bad that this mutates the original `headers` arg? i.e. should it be this?
```py
            headers = record.args["headers"] = {**record.args["headers"]}
```

discussion_comments.commented_code:
@@ -23,3 +25,15 @@ def setup_logging() -> None:
         _basic_config()
         logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)
         httpx_logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)
+
+
+class APIKeyFilter(logging.Filter):
+    @override
+    def filter(self, record: logging.LogRecord) -> bool:
+        if isinstance(record.args, dict) and "headers" in record.args:
+            if isinstance(record.args["headers"], dict):
+                logged_headers: Dict[str, Any] = record.args["headers"]
+                for header in logged_headers:

discussion_comments.comment_body:
Updated with your revisions, thanks! The logging docs for `filter` permits in-place modification:

> If deemed appropriate, the record may be modified in-place.

discussion_comments.comment_body:
I'm slightly nervous that this means we'll override any `Authorization` header that a user set which wouldn't be what they wanted.

I think we could restructure how this `_prepare_options()` method is called slightly to avoid this instead, I'll look into it.
